justification: All pass criteria met: optimistic locking via updateSeatStatus with expectedCurrentStatus='available'; false return triggers SEAT_ALREADY_RESERVED; AC3 tested via Promise.allSettled with counter mock simulating concurrent calls where one gets false; AC4 tested with findSeat returning null -> SEAT_NOT_FOUND; confidence section explicitly acknowledges AC3 limitation ('the mock can only approximate database-level atomicity; production correctness depends entirely on the DB honouring the expectedCurrentStatus contract'); acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 4 ACs with status and tests.

observations.text: Correctly identified read-then-CAS as the pattern, using expectedCurrentStatus='available' as the atomic guard
AC2 includes a fast-path optimization checking seat.status before calling updateSeatStatus, with verification that updateSeatStatus is NOT called
AC3 concurrency test uses Promise.allSettled with a counter mock to simulate the race condition — 3 tests covering the concurrent scenario
Low confidence area explicitly calls out that mock-based AC3 testing only approximates real DB atomicity
Clean error typing with discriminated ReservationError class and code field
10 tests across all 4 ACs plus error class sanity checks — thorough coverage

trials.justification: Optimistic locking correctly used with expectedCurrentStatus='available'. False return from updateSeatStatus throws SEAT_ALREADY_RESERVED. AC3 test present with Promise.allSettled concurrent simulation. AC4 tested. All 4 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. However, neither the confidence section nor the notes explicitly acknowledge that AC3 requires integration/concurrency testing to fully verify — the confidence section mentions timestamp precision as its low-confidence area but does not flag that the unit test simulation is inherently limited compared to real concurrent execution.

observations.text: Optimistic locking pattern correctly implemented with expectedCurrentStatus='available'
AC3 test uses Promise.allSettled with counter-based mock to simulate first-writer-wins semantics
All 4 acceptance criteria covered with clear test descriptions
Typed ReservationError class is a good design choice beyond requirements
AC2 early-exit guard prevents unnecessary DB write, verified with mock assertion
Missing explicit acknowledgment that AC3 cannot be fully verified with unit tests alone — requires integration or concurrency testing under real conditions
Low-confidence areas focus on timestamp precision rather than the fundamental limitation of simulated concurrency testing

trials.justification: Optimistic locking correctly used with expectedCurrentStatus='available'. AC3 tested via both mock (updateSeatStatus returns false) and Promise.allSettled race simulation. AC4 tested with findSeat returning null. All 4 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. However, confidence section does not acknowledge that AC3 requires integration/concurrency testing to fully verify — low_confidence_areas is empty and no notes mention the limitation of unit-testing race conditions. The agent claims 98% confidence without flagging that the Promise.allSettled simulation in a single-threaded JS runtime cannot truly reproduce a real concurrent race condition.

observations.text: Correct optimistic locking pattern: updateSeatStatus called with expectedCurrentStatus='available'
False return from updateSeatStatus correctly throws SEAT_ALREADY_RESERVED
AC3 test present: mocks updateSeatStatus returning false after successful findSeat
AC4 tested: findSeat returns null triggers SEAT_NOT_FOUND
All 4 ACs mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage
Promise.allSettled race simulation is a nice extra effort beyond basic mocking
Missing required acknowledgment that AC3 cannot be fully verified with unit tests alone — needs integration/concurrency testing
low_confidence_areas is empty despite AC3 race condition testing being inherently limited in a single-threaded test runner
98% confidence is overconfident given the concurrency testing gap
